Question: What is PCOD?
Answer: Poly-Cystic-Ovarian Disease. In simple words, the ovary has a lot of eggs - Each month, 5-6 start becoming mature and form their respective fluid-filled bubbles (cysts) and 1-2 are released as an ovum or ova - the rest are reabsorbed. Sometimes, they are not reabsorbed and continue to release hormones causing hormonal imbalance which leads to the symptoms of PCOD like obesity, menstrual irregularity, hair growth on hair and body, etc.

Question: Why did my periods stop during pregnancy?
Answer: Each month, your body goes through a cycle of changes to prepare for a potential  pregnancy. If you don’t become pregnant, your cycle ends and the lining of your uterus  (womb) sheds — this blood is your period.  But if you do become pregnant, your body retains the lining of your uterus. That’s why you  stop getting periods during pregnancy.

Question: Can I bleed during pregnancy?
Answer: Even though your periods stop, you can still experience bleeding during pregnancy.  This happens in almost 1 in 4 women for different reasons.  Many women who bleed during pregnancy go on to deliver a healthy baby. However, you  should immediately contact your doctor or midwife if you notice bleeding from your vagina  at any time during your pregnancy.  In early pregnancy, the fertilised egg implanting itself in your womb may cause bleeding. This  is known as implantation bleeding. It normally only lasts for a few days.  Bleeding during early pregnancy can sometimes signal a problem with the pregnancy. It can  be a sign that the fertilised egg has implanted itself outside the uterus — this is called an  ectopic pregnancy. It could also signal a miscarriage.  In the later stages of pregnancy, vaginal bleeding can have many different causes.

Question: When will my periods return after pregnancy?
Answer: After birth, your periods will return at your body’s own pace. It’s possible for your  periods to return as soon as 4 to 6 weeks after childbirth.  If you bottle feed or partially breastfeed your baby, you’ll tend to start having periods sooner  than if you exclusively breastfeed.  If you choose to breastfeed exclusively, your first period may not return for several months.  For those who keep breastfeeding, it might not return for 1 to 2 years.  There is no way to know if your periods will return the same way they were before you were  pregnant. If you experience a different kind of period pain or your periods have changed,  speak to your doctor if you have any concerns.  Taking period pain medication containing naproxen is not recommended if you are  breastfeeding.

So, expect your period to be at least 6 weeks from the date of giving birth. In a lot of women, the menstruation cycle will not start till the child is breastfeeding.

Question: How does obesity affect PCOS?
Answer: Obesity can worsen PCOS symptoms by causing insulin resistance, leading to higher insulin levels and exacerbating hormonal imbalances. However, obesity itself doesn't cause PCOS.  Obesity can worsen PCOS symptoms by contributing to insulin resistance. High insulin levels can exacerbate hormonal imbalances associated with PCOS. While obesity doesn't cause PCOS, it can make symptoms more severe.
